region_of_origin: China (Buddhist tradition); widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Braised Buddhist vegetarian stew with multiple plant-based ingredients including glass noodles, tofu puffs, mushrooms, dried lily buds, wood ear fungus, ginkgo nuts, lotus seeds, dried bean curd, carrots, goji berries, and red dates

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Lo Han Chai (Buddhist vegetarian stew). Closest parent would be a mixed vegetable stew or braised vegetable dish. No defensible exact URI available; null assigned per ontology honesty policy.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dishes — vegetable-based stew (closest parent; FoodEx2 composite dish category for mixed vegetable preparations)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Lo Han Chai. A00LC (composite mixed dish) used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Lo Han Chai or Buddhist vegetarian stew as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one bowl (250g), as typically served at dim sum restaurants and Buddhist vegetarian eateries in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Lo Han Chai (Buddhist vegetarian stew) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database entry for Lo Han Chai. Values represent a composite dish per 100g as prepared.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of bean thread vermicelli (glass noodles) and composite vegetable stew estimation — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021 (International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lo Han Chai as a composite dish. GI ~70 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being glass noodles (bean thread vermicelli). Published GI values for bean thread vermicelli range from 26 to 79 depending on preparation; the higher end (~70) is applied here to account for the braised preparation with sauce, which increases starch gelatinisation and glycemic response. GL per 250g bowl = (70 × 27.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 19.3. Confidence 0.9 — moderate-high estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 215mg/100g (538mg per 250g bowl) from vegetarian oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: One bowl provides approximately 538mg sodium (23% of the 2300mg daily limit); patients with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl or request reduced-sauce preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes at the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load ~19.3 per bowl, primarily from glass noodles (bean thread vermicelli) and natural sugars in red dates and vegetarian sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: The GL of ~19.3 per bowl is moderate-high; the 5.5g dietary fibre per bowl and the protein and fat from tofu puffs help moderate the glycemic response, but patients with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a half-bowl portion (~125g) or pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the overall glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.6g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and diverse plant-based phytonutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lo Han Chai is a cardioprotective dish choice — it is cholesterol-free, very low in saturated fat, and provides beta-glucans from wood ear fungus and shiitake mushrooms that may support LDL reduction; the primary cardiovascular caution is the moderate sodium content from fermented condiments, which should be monitored in patients with heart failure or sodium-restricted diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Multiple anti-inflammatory phytonutrients from 18 diverse plant ingredients including beta-glucans, zeaxanthin, quercetin, and ergocalciferol (Vitamin D from mushrooms)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The diverse plant ingredient profile provides genuine anti-inflammatory benefit for RA patients; the very low saturated fat content (0.6g/100g) and absence of animal products make this an excellent choice, though the moderate sodium from soy-based condiments should be noted for patients on corticosteroids with sodium sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lo Han Chai, also known as Buddha's Delight (罗汉斋, Luó Hàn Zhāi), is a traditional Chinese Buddhist vegetarian braised stew that holds deep cultural and religious significance. The dish is named after the 18 Luohan (arhats — enlightened monks in Buddhism) and traditionally contains 18 symbolic plant-based ingredients. In Singapore, it is widely served at dim sum restaurants, Buddhist temples, and vegetarian eateries, particularly during the Hungry Ghost Festival (seventh lunar month), the Nine Emperor Gods Festival (ninth lunar month), Chinese New Year, and the Vegetarian Festival. The dish is prepared by braising a diverse combination of ingredients including glass noodles (bean thread vermicelli), tofu puffs, shiitake mushrooms, wood ear fungus, dried lily buds, ginkgo nuts, lotus seeds, dried bean curd skin (fu chok), carrots, goji berries, and red dates in a seasoned broth of vegetarian oyster sauce, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The texture is soft and silky from the braising process, with the glass noodles absorbing the savoury-sweet sauce. Nutritionally, Lo Han Chai is one of the most diverse single dishes in Chinese cuisine — it is vegan, dairy-free, and provides 3g protein, 4g fat, 11g carbohydrate, and 2.2g dietary fibre per 100g (92 kcal/100g). A standard 250g bowl provides approximately 230 kcal, 5.5g fibre, 538mg sodium, and zero cholesterol. The dish is very low in saturated fat (0.6g/100g) and provides meaningful micronutrients including iron (1.5mg/100g), calcium (55mg/100g), potassium (180mg/100g), Vitamin A (40mcg RAE/100g), and Vitamin D (1.0mcg/100g from ergocalciferol in mushrooms). Clinically, the main concerns are moderate sodium from fermented condiments and a moderate-high glycemic load (~19.3 per bowl) from glass noodles. For cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients, the anti-inflammatory phytonutrient diversity and very low saturated fat profile make this an excellent plant-based dish choice.
meal_context: lunar new year eve vegetarian meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The dish is a well-documented traditional preparation with consistent ingredient profiles across sources. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor (glass noodles) using University of Sydney GI Database data, with confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite braised vegetable stew is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats). vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — this is a fully vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — no animal products present. GI value of 70 is an analogous estimate; the actual GI of Lo Han Chai as a composite dish has not been directly measured in a published clinical study. GL per serving calculated as: GI (70) × available carbohydrate per serving (27.5g from 11g/100g × 250g) / 100 = 19.25, rounded to 19.3. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest defensible parent anchor for composite mixed vegetable dish; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Lo Han Chai specifically.
